research.dimension: Nextcloud Talk (spreed) as an experience model for sovereign skcomms/LiveKit — conversation model, signaling/SFU, call escalation, recording, and the Talk Bot/webhook API for AI participants
keyFindings: A 'call' is NOT a separate entity — it is just a STATE on a conversation. The same `token` addresses chat, files, polls, and the live call. Escalation is `POST /ocs/v2.php/apps/spreed/api/v4/call/{token}` with a `flags` bitmask; leaving the call (`DELETE /call/{token}`) keeps you in the conversation. This single-token model is the central design insight: chat and call are one room, one identity, one history.

The in-call `flags` field is a bitmask: DISCONNECTED=0, IN_CALL=1, WITH_AUDIO=2, WITH_VIDEO=4, WITH_PHONE=8, WITH_SCREEN=16. `PUT /call/{token}` mutates flags mid-call to toggle audio/video/screenshare without renegotiating the room. `GET /call/{token}` returns participants with `sessionId` + `lastPing` for presence. 1:1 ring is a separate explicit step: `POST /call/{token}/ring/{attendeeId}`.

Room model (roomType enum): 1=one-to-one, 2=group, 3=public (guest-joinable via link), plus legacy 4=changelog/5=former-1:1. Access-control fields are all integer-enum state on the room object: `lobbyState` (webinar gate), `readOnly`, `listable` (discoverability scope), `hasPassword`, `sipEnabled`+`attendeePin` (dial-in), `messageExpiration` (TTL auto-delete in seconds), `mentionPermissions` (@all gate), `recordingConsent`. Permissions are a per-attendee bitmask (`attendeePermissions`/`callPermissions`/`defaultPermissions`).

Breakout rooms are modeled as CHILD rooms of a parent conversation: creating a breakout child auto-sets the parent lobby until started; deleting the parent cascades. This means 'sub-rooms' reuse the exact same room primitive rather than a new type.

Two signaling tiers: (1) INTERNAL signaling = mesh P2P over Nextcloud PHP long-polling, fine for ~2-4 people; (2) the High-Performance Backend (HPB) = `nextcloud-spreed-signaling`, a Go WebSocket server fronting Janus Gateway's VideoRoom plugin as the SFU. Clients NEVER talk to Janus directly — all SDP offer/answer/ICE is relayed through the Go signaling server, which hides the MCU entirely.

HPB scaling stack: NATS as the inter-instance message bus, etcd (or DNS SRV) for service discovery/clustering, and a separate 'signaling proxy' component where each proxy fronts exactly one Janus server — so you scale media by adding proxies, with GeoIP-based MCU selection to route users to the nearest media node. gRPC interconnects signaling instances into one 'virtual' server for HA/failover.

Signaling WebSocket protocol is a clean JSON envelope `{id, type, <type>:{...}}` with message types: `hello` (auth via backend ticket/URL+params → returns sessionid/resumeid/userid), `room` (join/leave by roomid+sessionid, empty roomid = leave), `message` (recipient.type = session|user|room|call carrying WebRTC `offer`/`answer`/`candidate`/`requestoffer`), `control` (moderator mute/hangup/dtmf), `bye`. Publisher sends offer with `roomType:'video'|'screen'`, audiocodec=opus, videocodec=vp9,vp8,h264; subscribers send `requestoffer` to pull a stream. Screen share is just `roomType:'screen'` — a second publisher track on the same session, not a separate mechanism.

Backend trust between PHP and the signaling/recording servers is a SHARED-SECRET HMAC: every backend request carries a random nonce + HMAC-SHA256(nonce+body, secret) checksum. Same pattern used for recording callbacks (`/recording/backend`).

Recording is a SEPARATE headless service (nextcloud/recording) that JOINS THE CALL AS A REAL PARTICIPANT (headless browser) to capture composited audio/video/screenshare — it is not an SFU server-side mux. Moderator triggers `POST /recording/{token}`; finished file is pushed back via `POST /recording/{token}/store` with HMAC validation. `recordingConsent` can gate joining. Same join-as-bot pattern is exactly how an AI teammate would attend a call.

Talk Bot API (capability `bots-v1`, since NC 27.1) is the headline borrow: bots are installed ONLY via CLI `occ talk:bot:install <name> <url> <secret> [--feature webhook|response|event|reaction]` (no web UI, for security). Inbound = ActivityStreams 2.0 webhook (Create/Note for messages, Like/Undo for reactions, Join/Leave for lifecycle) with actor/object/target(room). Outbound = `POST /bot/{token}/message` (+ replyTo, referenceId, silent) and `POST|DELETE /bot/{token}/reaction/{messageId}`. Every direction is authenticated with HMAC-SHA256 over a RANDOM header + body using the per-bot shared secret; headers `X-Nextcloud-Talk-Signature`/`-Random`/`-Backend` inbound and `X-Nextcloud-Talk-Bot-Signature`/`-Random` outbound. 32k char limit, 429 rate-limited.

Bots can also run IN-PROCESS as a Nextcloud app via `nextcloudapp://$APPID` + the `events` feature flag (value 4) listening to `OCA\Talk\Events\BotInvokeEvent` — zero HTTP overhead. The real AI bots (call_summary_bot, Summary Bot / context_chat) use the LLM provider abstraction (Assistant) to post chat summaries on-demand or scheduled, and post a post-call summary listing participants + extracted tasks. Transcription is produced via the OpenAI/LLM provider on the recording file and delivered as a sibling file the moment the recording ends.

Federation: a conversation on server A can include users from server B via cloud-ID invite (OCM/Open Cloud Mesh share). The home server proxies chat AND now calls for the remote participant; servers trust each other via shared-secret. It is owner-centric proxying (the room lives on the host server, remote users are federated attendees) — conceptually identical to your skcomms 'owner-centric, not full replication' envelope model.

Rich object / mention system: chat messages carry a `message` template string + a `parameters` dict of 'Rich Object Strings' (typed objects: user, call, file, geo-location, deck-card, etc.). Mentions, file shares, location shares, and polls are all the SAME mechanism — typed parameters rendered client-side — rather than bespoke message types. Polls, reactions (emoji on messageId), and file shares are first-class but ride this one extensible envelope.
whatToBorrow: The single-token room model: make a skcomms 'room' the ONE addressable entity (your FQID) that holds chat history AND the live LiveKit call state. A call = `in_call` state flag on the room, not a new object. Reuse the SAME room token as the LiveKit room name so chat and call share identity/history/ACL.

The in-call flags bitmask (IN_CALL=1, AUDIO=2, VIDEO=4, SCREEN=16). LiveKit already exposes per-participant track publication; map it to this bitmask in your room presence so 'who is on, with what media' is one cheap GET, mirroring `GET /call/{token}`.

The Talk Bot webhook contract verbatim for 'Lumina in the room': ActivityStreams 2.0 inbound (Create/Like/Join), `POST /bot/{token}/message` outbound, HMAC-SHA256(random+body, per-bot-secret) BOTH directions with explicit signature/random/backend headers. This is a battle-tested, minimal, capauth-compatible contract — your capauth-signed envelope already does the signing half; adopt the random-nonce+HMAC header shape and the Create/Note/Like event vocabulary so any external/AI participant integrates uniformly.

The dual bot mode: webhook (external AI service) AND in-process (`events` flag, BotInvokeEvent). For skcomms: a Lumina bot can be a remote webhook OR an in-process skcomms lane handler — same identity, two transports. The `silent` flag on bot posts (suppress notifications) is worth copying for agent chatter.

Recording/agent-as-participant pattern: have Lumina (and a recording/transcription agent) JOIN the LiveKit room as a normal participant via a LiveKit token rather than tapping the SFU server-side. LiveKit's egress/agents framework is the direct analog of nextcloud/recording's headless-join. Use the HMAC-callback + `recordingConsent` gate pattern for compliance.

The HMAC shared-secret backend-trust pattern (random nonce + HMAC-SHA256(nonce+body)) for every server-to-server callback (signaling auth, recording store, bot post). Even with capauth/PGP, this lightweight nonce-HMAC is the right shape for high-frequency machine callbacks where full PGP is overkill.

The Rich Object String envelope: ONE message schema = `text` + `parameters{type:object}` to represent mentions, file shares, geo-location, polls, and call cards. Build skcomms messages on this single extensible typed-parameter model instead of per-feature message types — it future-proofs reactions/polls/location/agent-action-cards.
Breakout rooms as child-of-parent rooms (lobby gating + cascade) — reuse your room primitive for sub-rooms / spaces instead of a new type, exactly matching your SK Spaces concept.

Lobby + listable + readOnly + messageExpiration as integer-enum room state for access control — a compact, serializable room ACL model that maps cleanly onto capauth scopes and your public/guest funnel-ingress conf-call plan.

The signaling 'requestoffer' subscriber pull model and `roomType:'screen'` as just-another-publisher-track — informs how you expose screenshare in skchat: a second published track on the same participant session, surfaced via the same flags bitmask.
recommendationsForUs: Define a skcomms Room as the single FQID entity carrying {chat history, ACL, in_call flags, participant presence}; make the LiveKit room name == the skcomms room token so escalating chat→call is a state transition, not a new object. Mirror Talk's `POST/PUT/DELETE call/{token}` as state ops on the room.

Specify a 'skcomms Bot API v1' that copies Talk's contract: ActivityStreams-2.0-shaped inbound events (message=Create/Note, reaction=Like/Undo, lifecycle=Join/Leave) over a webhook, outbound `POST /bot/{room}/message` + reaction endpoints, all HMAC-SHA256(random+body, per-bot-secret). Register Lumina as the first bot. This gives you a uniform, language-agnostic way for ANY agent to participate, independent of MCP/Hermes.

Implement Lumina-in-the-call as a LiveKit participant (LiveKit Agents framework + a server-minted token), NOT an SFU-side hook — directly analogous to nextcloud/recording's headless join. Reuse the same agent-join path for a transcription/summary agent that posts a post-call summary message (participants + extracted tasks) into the room chat, matching call_summary_bot.

Adopt the Rich Object String pattern as the skcomms message schema (text + typed parameters) so mentions, file sends (you already have file_send), polls, geo-location, and 'agent action cards' all ride one extensible envelope rather than bespoke types.

Keep your capauth/PGP for human identity + initial room/bot provisioning, but add the lightweight random-nonce + HMAC-SHA256 shared-secret pattern for high-frequency machine callbacks (agent posts, recording store, presence). This is the proven Talk/HPB trust shape and avoids PGP overhead per message.

Model access control as compact room state enums (lobbyState, listable, readOnly, hasPassword, messageExpiration, recordingConsent) mapped to capauth scopes — this gives the sovereign conf-call plan its guest/lobby/public-funnel gates with a serializable, federation-friendly ACL.
For SK Spaces, implement sub-rooms as child-of-parent rooms (lobby + cascade-delete) reusing the room primitive, exactly as Talk does breakout rooms.

For your owner-centric federation: model remote agents/users as federated attendees on a host-owned room (host proxies chat+call), trusting peer servers via shared-secret/capauth — this matches both Talk federation and your existing skfed envelope philosophy; do NOT full-replicate rooms.
bestPractices2027: Treat chat and call as one entity addressed by one token — escalate in place, never spin a separate meeting object (Talk's core model, now standard).

Hide the SFU entirely behind a signaling layer: clients exchange only abstract offer/answer/requestoffer; the server picks/relays the MCU (Janus VideoRoom). LiveKit already enforces this — keep clients SFU-agnostic.
Scale media horizontally with one-MCU-per-proxy + GeoIP selection + NATS/etcd clustering for HA; route users to nearest media node. Mirror with LiveKit's multi-node/region routing.
Authenticate ALL server-to-server callbacks with random-nonce + HMAC-SHA256(nonce+body, shared-secret) and explicit signature/random/backend headers.
Run AI/recording as real call participants (headless join), not server-side taps — gate with explicit recording consent state on the room.
Install bots via CLI/admin only (never a web-exposed self-serve), each with its own per-bot shared secret and feature flags (webhook/response/event/reaction).
Carry mentions/files/polls/locations as typed Rich-Object parameters on one message schema; let clients render — keeps the wire format stable as features grow.
Deliver AI transcription as a sibling file the moment recording ends, and post LLM call/chat summaries (participants + tasks) back into the same room via the bot API.
pitfalls: Internal mesh signaling does NOT scale past ~2-4 participants — Talk requires the HPB (Janus SFU) for real group calls. Lesson: never ship P2P-mesh as your group-call path; LiveKit SFU is mandatory (you already have it).

Recording-as-headless-browser-participant is resource-heavy (a full browser per recorded call) and is the historically fragile/ops-heavy piece. Prefer LiveKit server-side egress where possible instead of a literal headless browser.

Bot security hinges entirely on correct HMAC verification (constant-time, case-insensitive hex compare of signature over RANDOM+body). A naive compare or skipping verification = anyone can impersonate the bot or inject messages. Web-UI bot install is deliberately disabled — don't expose self-serve bot registration.
The 32k-char message limit and 429 rate-limiting on bot posts mean a chatty AI agent must chunk/throttle; design Lumina's posting cadence around this.

HPB clustering (NATS+etcd+gRPC+signaling proxies+coturn) is genuinely complex to operate; the multi-node story has real setup/firewall (zone) footguns — budget ops effort, and lean on LiveKit's managed clustering rather than rebuilding Janus topology.

Federation across servers historically lagged calls behind chat and has trust/secret-management overhead; treat federated calls as a maturing feature and keep the host-owned-room (proxy) model rather than attempting symmetric replication.

A call being 'just state on a conversation' means call lifecycle bugs surface as stale presence — Talk relies on `lastPing` timeouts to garbage-collect dead sessions; you must implement equivalent ping/TTL reaping or rooms will show phantom participants.

research.dimension: WebRTC group calling / SFU best practices for 2027 — LiveKit-centric (1:1↔group escalation, screenshare/simulcast/SVC, scaling, TURN/ICE, server-side egress recording) vs mediasoup/Janus
keyFindings: Hybrid topology is the 2027 default, NOT a binary choice: P2P for the 1:1 leg (sub-500ms, zero server media cost), SFU the moment a 3rd participant or screenshare joins. P2P mesh is dead beyond ~4 peers — a 6-person mesh forces each peer to upload 5 streams (quadratic), drains a mid-range Android in <1hr. Industry rule of thumb: 2=P2P, 3-12=SFU, hundreds=SFU+CDN/MCU.

LiveKit's architectural answer to on-the-fly escalation is that you don't actually escalate transports — you put everyone in a LiveKit room from the start and let the SFU handle 1:1 and N-way identically. A '1:1' is just a 2-participant room; adding a 3rd is a join, not a renegotiation. This sidesteps the painful P2P->SFU media-path swap that mediasoup/Janus force you to hand-roll at the app layer.

LiveKit's killer bandwidth feature is the Dynacast (publisher-side) + Adaptive Stream (subscriber-side) closed loop. Dynacast stops encoding any simulcast/SVC layer with zero subscribers; Adaptive Stream uses a browser ResizeObserver on the actual rendered <video> pixel size and asks the SFU for the closest-matching layer. Documented result: a 25-person room drops from ~45Mbps to ~4Mbps (92% reduction). mediasoup/Janus require you to wire all of this yourself.

Simulcast vs SVC in 2027: simulcast (VP8/H.264) is still the cross-browser safe default (mandatory if you have Safari publishers); VP9/AV1 SVC is the Chromium/AI-agent/broadcast choice (single encode, server extracts layers, more publisher-efficient). LiveKit supports multi-codec simulcast so ONE publisher serves both SVC and simulcast subscribers from the same room.

Recording is done server-side via the standalone LiveKit Egress service (Go + GStreamer + headless Chrome), NOT in-client. Four request types: RoomComposite (whole-room rendered via an HTML layout template in Chrome), Web (record any URL, room-independent), TrackComposite (sync one participant's audio+video, e.g. their camera+screenshare), and Track (raw single-track export). Outputs: MP4/OGG/WebM, HLS(TS segments), RTMP(s), SRT, WebSocket, + JPEG thumbnails. Destinations: S3-compatible, Azure Blob, GCS, Alibaba OSS.

Egress is CPU-heavy and the cost model is explicit: RoomComposite/Web = 3.0 CPU units each (they each spin up a full Chrome + encode), TrackComposite = 2.0, Track = 1.0. Egress MUST share the same Redis address as the LiveKit server, runs as separate worker instances, and autoscales (K8s Helm chart, CPU-based). Auto-record: set the egress field on CreateRoom to capture room-composite + per-track automatically on room creation.

Scaling model differs fundamentally: LiveKit = Redis-coordinated mesh cluster where any participant connects to any node and the cluster routes media between nodes hosting other participants — a room transparently spans multiple servers with automatic cascading/region placement. mediasoup = manual (~500 consumers/worker, you assign rooms to workers and wire pipeToRouter yourself). Janus = single-instance to hundreds, then app-layer routing across instances. LiveKit removes the 'which worker/node hosts this room' app logic the others impose.

TURN/ICE: LiveKit ships an EMBEDDED TURN server (no separate coturn needed for most deploys). For maximum reachability through corporate firewalls, enable TURN/TLS on 443 with a CA-signed cert (self-signed certs do NOT work; a separate TURN subdomain+cert is required). TURN/UDP can also be advertised on 443 for some firewall scenarios. External coturn is supported but mid-2025 GitHub issues show external-IP/TLS integration is fiddly — the embedded TURN is the lower-friction path.

LiveKit Agents framework (1.5+) lets any Python/Node program join a room as a first-class realtime participant — same media plane as humans, so an AI teammate can see screenshare, hear active speaker, and publish back. Native MCP tool integration (one line), semantic turn-detection transformer to reduce interruptions, and a built-in SIP/telephony gateway. 2025-26 production latency medians: 1.4-1.7s, p99 3-5s; ~450ms achievable with full streaming + co-located regions + pre-warmed contexts.

Access control is pure JWT: a token encodes identity + grants (room, canPublish, canSubscribe, roomCreate, etc.). RoomCreate grant auto-creates the room on connect — so on-the-fly group calls need no pre-provisioning step; mint a token and join.
whatToBorrow: LiveKit Egress RoomComposite with a CUSTOM HTML layout template — we already render layouts in our skchat/conf-calls UI; point egress at our own template URL (default template port 7980) so recordings match the live layout exactly. Set duration limits (file 1hr / stream 90min / segment 3hr defaults) to bound runaway jobs.

Auto-egress via the egress field on CreateRoom — flip on for any room flagged 'record' so a RoomComposite MP4 + per-track files land in S3 (or our Nextcloud/MinIO-compatible store) automatically without a separate start-egress call.

Dynacast + Adaptive Stream — make sure adaptiveStream:true and dynacast:true are set in our Flutter/JS RoomOptions. Wire our tile UI state (pinned / thumbnail / off-screen) into the layer-selection hint; the cited 30-60% egress cut on many-tile rooms and 92% on a 25-person room is free money on our home GPU/bandwidth budget.

TrackComposite egress for the 'record just the presenter + their screenshare' case (set screen_share:true on ParticipantEgress) — cleaner than RoomComposite when we want a single clean asset per speaker rather than the whole grid.

LiveKit Agents framework to formalize the existing LUMINA-as-teammate pattern: join calls as a real room participant (we already do CoT/TAK LUMINA), use semantic turn-detection to stop the agent talking over people, and native MCP so our skcapstone MCP tools are callable mid-call with one line.

Embedded TURN/TLS on 443 — drop any separate coturn dependency for the conf-calls funnel ingress; one CA cert on a turn subdomain gives corporate-firewall traversal. Keep the cert in our existing CF/SKStacks cert flow.
Multi-codec simulcast config: default VP8/H.264 simulcast for broad client support, enable AV1/VP9 SVC for agent rooms and Chromium-only sovereign clients to cut publisher upload on the home uplink.
config-sample.yaml from livekit/livekit as the canonical reference for turn.tls_port/external-ip/redis blocks — clone it rather than hand-writing config.
recommendationsForUs: Adopt the 'everyone in a LiveKit room, always' model for skchat/sovereign-conf-calls — do NOT build a bespoke P2P->SFU escalation handshake. Treat 1:1 as a 2-person room; 'escalate to group' = mint+hand out a token and the new peer joins. This eliminates the renegotiation class of bugs and matches what LiveKit is built for. (Our skfed/skcomms capauth-signed envelope still governs WHO gets a token; LiveKit governs the media.)

Stand up the LiveKit Egress service as a dedicated worker (NOT on the .100/5060 GPU box — egress is CPU+Chrome heavy and the MEMORY.md note says don't add 5060 load). Point it at the SAME Redis the LiveKit server uses. Budget ~3 CPU per concurrent RoomComposite; if home CPU is tight, prefer TrackComposite (2 CPU) or Track (1 CPU) per-participant recording and stitch later.

Wire RoomComposite egress to a custom template that reuses our existing conf-call layout, write recordings to our S3-compatible store (MinIO/Nextcloud), and gate playback through capauth — this gives sovereign, owner-controlled recordings with no third-party cloud.

Replace/avoid any standalone coturn for conf-calls with LiveKit's embedded TURN/TLS on 443 behind the funnel ingress; provision a turn.<domain> CA cert via our CF/SKStacks pipeline. Document the 443-must-be-advertised gotcha (turn.tls_port=443 when no LB) in runbooks/ alongside the existing sksso-cloudflared notes.

Confirm adaptiveStream + dynacast are ON in the skchat-app RoomOptions and bind tile lifecycle (pin/thumbnail/offscreen) to layer hints before we scale past ~6 participants — this is the single biggest bandwidth lever on our residential uplink.

Formalize LUMINA-in-call via the LiveKit Agents framework rather than ad-hoc bot plumbing: gives semantic turn-detection (no talking over Chef), native MCP so skcapstone tools work mid-call, and same-media-plane access to screenshare/active-speaker. This supersedes hand-rolled call_peer/initiate_call glue for the agent participant.

Keep LiveKit, don't switch to mediasoup/Janus: LiveKit's ceiling (Redis mesh cluster, native egress, Agents+SIP, Dynacast/Adaptive, embedded TURN) covers every stated goal with the least app-layer code. mediasoup would only win if we needed total signalling control / strict HIPAA isolation; Janus only if we needed its all-in-one SIP+RTSP+SFU box. Neither advantage applies to our sovereign group-video+screenshare+record goal.

Record the cost/latency expectations in MEMORY/runbooks: egress CPU units (3/3/2/1), the 92% adaptive-stream bandwidth claim, and the Agents latency reality (1.4-1.7s median, 450ms only with co-located+pre-warmed) so future agents size hardware and set UX expectations correctly.
bestPractices2027: Hybrid by default: P2P only for true 1:1 where latency/cost matter; SFU from the 3rd participant or any screenshare onward; CDN/MCU tail only for hundreds-of-viewers broadcast.

Always pair simulcast/SVC with subscriber-driven layer selection (Adaptive Stream / Dynacast equivalent) and feed UI viewport state into the SFU's layer hint — this is the highest-ROI optimization and routinely cuts SFU egress 30-60% (up to 92% in large grids).
Default to VP8/H.264 simulcast for cross-browser (Safari) safety; switch to VP9/AV1 SVC only for Chromium-only or AI-agent rooms where the single-encode publisher savings pay off.
Do recording server-side via egress workers, never in the client; isolate egress on dedicated/autoscaled CPU instances sharing the SFU's Redis, and bound jobs with duration limits.
Prefer per-track / track-composite egress when you need clean per-participant assets or want to avoid the cost of a full Chrome-rendered room composite.
Enable TURN/TLS on 443 with a CA-signed cert for maximum firewall traversal; advertise the right external IP/port and verify the relay path actually engages (a common silent failure).

Mint short-lived JWTs with least-privilege grants per participant (canPublish/canSubscribe/roomCreate scoped per session) and use RoomCreate-on-connect to avoid a separate provisioning round-trip for ad-hoc group calls.
Treat AI participants as first-class room members through an agents framework (real media plane, semantic turn-detection, MCP tools) rather than bolting a bot onto signalling.
pitfalls: P2P mesh beyond ~4 participants: quadratic upload, mobile battery/CPU collapse — never use it for group calls.

RoomComposite egress is expensive (full headless Chrome + encode, 3 CPU/job): running several concurrently on a small/GPU-shared box will starve it; co-locating egress with the LLM/ComfyUI GPU box is explicitly the wrong move given our 5060 VRAM/CPU constraints.
Egress that doesn't share the SAME Redis as the LiveKit server simply won't receive jobs — a silent misconfig.

Self-signed TURN certs do NOT work; TURN needs its own subdomain + CA-signed cert and the correct external-IP/443 advertisement — external coturn integration has documented external-IP/TLS footguns (livekit issues #3826/#3971/#3595).

Forgetting to enable adaptiveStream/dynacast leaves you paying full multi-Mbps egress per subscriber even when tiles are tiny or off-screen — the 92% savings only happen if explicitly enabled and UI-wired.
Assuming SVC everywhere: Safari/older clients lack reliable VP9/AV1 SVC; an SVC-only room can leave those publishers/subscribers degraded — keep a simulcast fallback.

Expecting sub-second AI-agent call latency by default: production medians are 1.4-1.7s; 450ms requires co-located regions + streaming-at-every-stage + pre-warmed contexts, which our distributed home topology (.100/.158/.41) may not achieve without tuning.
No SFU (LiveKit/mediasoup/Janus) auto-selects layers for you out of the box — subscription/layer policy must be configured; default behavior can over-send.

research.dimension: Chat/Comms UX & Modular Surfaces (2026-2027 best-of-breed) — Signal, Telegram, Element/Matrix, Discord, Slack, Teams, WhatsApp, Nextcloud Talk
keyFindings: ESCALATION (1:1 -> group -> voice/video) has converged on the 'Call Link' pattern as the 2026 best practice. Signal's Calls tab lets you create a reusable, optionally-named call link with no group required, default admin-approval-to-join, raise-hand queue, and aggregated emoji reactions (75-participant cap). Nextcloud Talk adds lobby/waiting rooms + breakout rooms over WebRTC E2E. This decouples 'a call' from 'a chat thread' — the call is a first-class object you share by link, which is exactly what skchat's Sovereign Conf Calls / shareable-link work is building toward.

MESSAGE UX is now a settled baseline that you cannot ship without: swipe-to-reply (Discord swipe right-to-left on a message), long-press/double-tap reaction tray with 6 suggested + overflow (Signal double-tap-to-edit), edit window (Signal: 24h, max 10 edits, edit-history indicator), threads as a distinct surface, and reactions that are aggregated/animated. Telegram added in-call ephemeral comments+reactions that float on the call screen (groups <1000).

THREADS have a clear winner model: Element explicitly rebuilt 'Threads 2.0' copying Discord+Slack rather than the old Matrix threads — subscribe/unsubscribe per-thread, auto or manual notification subscription, drafts inside threads, media, permalinks. Matrix Rust SDK exposes Room::list_threads(). Discord adds per-channel 'channel details' showing Media/Files/Links/Threads as filtered sub-views of one channel — threads + scoped media browser in one panel.

NAVIGATION has split into dedicated top-level tabs everywhere: Discord moved Servers / Messages / Notifications / You into separate tabs (swipe left/right on the 'You Bar' flips DMs<->current server). Slack's 2026 redesign replaced the unified sidebar with Home / DMs / Activity / Later / More views. The 'Activity' tab (consolidated mentions+threads+reactions+app notifications) and 'Later'/save-for-later are now standard top-level surfaces. This is the IA backbone to copy.

MODULAR / CUSTOMIZABLE SURFACES — the exact thing the user described — is real and shipping. Slack lets users pin tools/tabs to the sidebar in one click, push the rest into 'More', AND share custom sidebar sections (incl. private channels) via link. Discord lets users Favorite channels + toggle which previews show. The user's 'swipe-up toolbar reveals all enabled sub-apps' maps directly to the 2026-dominant BOTTOM-SHEET pattern (draggable panel anchored to screen bottom — Telegram/Apple Maps standard) used as an app-drawer of enabled modules, plus Samsung One UI 8.5's interactive swipe-up app drawer as the gesture precedent.

EXTENSIBILITY: the platform-of-platforms model is the moat. Telegram Mini Apps (bug-report/feature-request/sticker creation all run inside the app without leaving it), Nextcloud Talk's Smart Picker + per-conversation app integrations (Files/Calendar/Deck/Maps/Whiteboard appear in the call/chat sidebar), Slack canvas embedded inside huddles + workflows embedded inside canvas. Sub-apps are surfaced contextually inside the conversation, not as separate destinations.

COMMUNITY/SCALE structure (relevant for skchat groups/federation): WhatsApp Communities = parent container linking up to 50 subgroups + a one-way Announcement Channel auto-joined by all members (broadcast to 5,000). Matrix Spaces (redesigned in Element X) group rooms, distinguish internal vs external spaces, and filter by unread. This is the hierarchy: Space/Community > rooms/subgroups > threads.
