research.dimension: Real-time collaborative document editing with an AI agent as a first-class collaborator (CRDT/OT, live-doc-in-chat, agent-as-peer patterns) — architecture recommendation for a Flutter + LLM (qwen/Lumina) stack
keyFindings: The defining 2026/2027 pattern is the AI agent as a SERVER-SIDE CRDT peer, not a client-side bolt-on. Electric's reference build (electric-sql/collaborative-ai-editor) opens its own Yjs document on the server and connects to the SAME transport as humans, presenting via the standard Yjs awareness protocol as a named participant ('Electra') with a moving cursor and status (thinking/composing/idle). This is the cleanest blueprint for what Chef wants.

The hardest technical truth: a rich-text CRDT is a DATA STRUCTURE, not a string. You CANNOT have the LLM emit a markdown/text diff and patch it. You must convert streaming model tokens into native CRDT ops incrementally (streaming markdown parser: `**bold**`->bold mark, `## h`->heading node). Treating the doc as text is the #1 architecture mistake.

Agent edits must be anchored by RELATIVE POSITIONS (Yjs relative positions / Loro cursors), not integer offsets. Electric's agent uses a read->locate->edit tool loop (get_document_snapshot, search_text returning stable match-handles backed by Yjs relative positions, place_cursor, insert_text, start_streaming_edit with modes continue/insert/rewrite). Relative anchors survive concurrent human edits; offsets do not.

Two opposing UX philosophies exist and BOTH are valid: (1) Electric = agent edits the doc DIRECTLY and live, visible as a cursor (fast, feels collaborative); (2) the arxiv 2026 study (2509.11826) = agent NEVER edits text directly, responds ONLY via comments/suggestions with Append/Replace/Approve gates, because 'edits carry social meaning.' The study found 23% of users felt loss of control with autonomous edits, and AI-initiated suggestions got ignored when they overwhelmed. Recommendation: default to suggestion/diff mode, allow opt-in direct mode.

CRDT library landscape 2026: Yjs is the production default (~920K weekly downloads, 17K stars, ~18kB gzip, no WASM). Loro wins encoding size (68kB vs Yjs 160kB vs Automerge 250kB for same doc; 2-5x smaller) and is fast, but younger ecosystem. Automerge has Git-like DAG history/branching but ~320kB bundle. For rich text, Yjs (XmlFragment + Y.RelativePosition) and Loro (Peritext-based rich text + native movable tree) are the rich-text-capable ones.

OT vs CRDT 2026 verdict: OT still wins for centralized server-authoritative SaaS (memory: OT ~2MB vs CRDT 34-66MB for 10k nodes, a 17-33x difference from tombstones). CRDT wins for offline-first, P2P, and MULTI-PEER including AI agents. Since the SK stack is sovereign/federated/offline-tolerant with multiple agent+human peers, CRDT (Yjs/Loro) is the right call, not OT.

AI agents write at 1000-4000 WPM (25-100x human speed), which floods both OT servers and CRDT metadata. Mitigation pattern (Taskade): operation batching AT THE AGENT LAYER + rate-limited broadcast. Do not stream every token as a separate CRDT op uncompacted.

Transport is converging on persistent, resumable, offset-addressable streams. Electric uses Durable Streams (HTTP, catch-up from last offset, no custom WebSocket reconnection): 3 streams per doc (doc updates / awareness / chat). Self-hosted classic path is Hocuspocus (y-websocket server) which now has AI-editor demos with Tiptap+Gemini.

Flutter has NO mature native-Dart rich-text CRDT. The proven path is the Rust core: y-crdt/yrs (binary-compatible Yjs port, has yffi C-FFI) or loro-rs, wrapped via flutter_rust_bridge. AppFlowy is the existence proof: Flutter UI -> appflowy_backend events -> Rust dart-ffi crate -> yrs. This is exactly the FFI shape the SK stack already uses elsewhere.

Live-doc-in-chat-room integration: a 'room' = a chat thread bound 1:1 to a document id; the same room broadcasts both chat messages and CRDT awareness. Self-host options: Etherpad (embeddable iframe + HTTP API, Nextcloud Talk integration via Ownpad/nextpad exists), Nextcloud Text (server-authoritative, weaker for agent-as-peer), or block editors BlockNote/Tiptap over Yjs (best for a Notion-style block model + agent ops).
whatToBorrow: electric-sql/collaborative-ai-editor (GitHub) — read it directly. The agent tool schema (get_document_snapshot / search_text / place_cursor / insert_text / start_streaming_edit{mode,format}) is a ready-to-adapt MCP-style toolset for Lumina/qwen. Adapt these as skchat MCP tools backed by a yrs document handle.

The 'streaming markdown -> CRDT nodes' incremental parser pattern — borrow this so qwen/Lumina output becomes real bold/heading/list nodes, not escaped text. Pair with Tiptap/ProseMirror or BlockNote schema.

Yjs Awareness protocol for agent presence: give Lumina an awareness entry with {user:{name:'Lumina',color}, cursor:relPos, status:'composing'} so she renders as a real cursor in the Flutter client like any peer.
Y.RelativePosition (yrs: yrs::RelativePosition / loro cursor) for ALL agent edit anchoring — search_text returns stable handles, agent edits survive concurrent human typing.

Suggestion/diff mode from arxiv 2509.11826: agent edits as a CRDT-tracked 'suggestion' overlay (separate Y.Map of pending changes or marks) with Append/Replace/Approve human gate + side-by-side preview. Make this the DEFAULT; direct-edit is opt-in per room.
Taskade's agent-layer operation batching + rate-limited broadcast to stop the 1000-4000 WPM agent from flooding the doc; coalesce token bursts into periodic CRDT updates and compact server-side.
Hocuspocus (self-hosted y-websocket) + Tiptap as the fastest path to a working web prototype before committing the Flutter/yrs FFI work; there are 2026 Tiptap+Hocuspocus+LLM AI-editor demos to copy.
AppFlowy's Flutter<->yrs dart-ffi architecture as the template for the Flutter client core (flutter_rust_bridge over yrs).
recommendationsForUs: Adopt CRDT, not OT: the SK stack is sovereign/federated/multi-peer/offline-tolerant — exactly CRDT's strong case. Pick Yjs/yrs as the primary (max ecosystem + binary-compatible Rust core for Flutter); keep Loro as a watch item for its 2-5x smaller encoding (matters for mobile sync + storing agent edit history in skmem-pg).

Architecture: ONE Rust CRDT core (yrs) shared by server and Flutter via flutter_rust_bridge. Server runs a Lumina/qwen 'doc agent' as a headless yrs peer on .158/.41 alongside skchat-telegram-opus. Transport = a resumable stream per room; if reusing existing skchat WebRTC/SFU+Nostr rails, run y-protocol sync messages over a dedicated data channel/relay, with offset catch-up so federated peers and an offline laptop reconcile.

Bind doc<->room: a skchat room id maps to a Yjs doc id. The room carries 3 logical channels (doc updates, awareness, chat) — model this in skcomms' capauth-signed envelope so every CRDT op AND agent edit is signed by the per-agent identity key (reuse the skcomms agent-signing-key fix). This gives you an audit trail of exactly which edits were Lumina's.

Make Lumina a real awareness peer: name + color + live cursor + status(thinking/composing/idle) in the Flutter client so co-drafting feels like a person is in the doc. Wire her prompt input from the chat pane; selection/cursor context from humans is encoded as relative anchors and attached to her prompt so 'rewrite this paragraph' targets the right node.

Default to SUGGESTION/DIFF mode (arxiv finding): Lumina's edits land as a tracked-changes overlay with Approve/Reject + side-by-side preview; promote to live direct-edit only when a human toggles 'co-write' for that room. This directly avoids the 23% loss-of-control and ignored-suggestion failure modes.

Expose the agent edit loop as skchat MCP tools mirroring Electric's set (doc_snapshot, search_text->relpos handle, place_cursor, insert/replace, start_streaming_edit) backed by yrs; route qwen3.6 (.100:8082) for bulk drafting and Opus for high-stakes rewrites via the existing model_route/SKGateway picker.

Build order: (1) prototype on web with Tiptap+Hocuspocus+qwen to validate UX cheaply; (2) port the CRDT core to yrs+flutter_rust_bridge in skchat-app following AppFlowy's pattern; (3) add suggestion-mode + capauth-signed ops + skmem-pg persistence of doc snapshots. Don't build Flutter-native CRDT from scratch — there's no mature Dart option.

Persistence: store yrs update logs + periodic snapshots in skmem-pg (you already have the docs table + AGE graph); compact server-side to keep tombstone bloat down, and you get versioning/branching for free as a base layer (consider Automerge ONLY if you later need Git-like branch/cherry-pick of agent drafts).
pitfalls: Treating the doc as a string and patching LLM text diffs — breaks rich-text CRDT structure; you MUST stream tokens as CRDT ops.
Anchoring agent edits by integer offsets — they corrupt under concurrent human typing; use relative positions only.
Letting the agent edit directly by default — arxiv study: 23% felt loss of control, autonomous triggers produced comment floods that users then ignored. Gate it.

Streaming every LLM token as an uncompacted CRDT update — 1000-4000 WPM agent floods the wire and balloons tombstone metadata (CRDT already uses 17-33x more memory than OT). Batch at the agent layer + server compaction.
Picking OT for a sovereign/federated/offline stack — OT needs a central authoritative server and loses the P2P/offline/multi-agent merge you actually want.
Expecting a turnkey Flutter CRDT — there isn't one; budget for the yrs/flutter_rust_bridge FFI core (AppFlowy proves it works but it's real work).
Loro's ecosystem immaturity — great encoding/perf but fewer editor bindings and integration recipes; don't bet the primary path on it yet.

Nextcloud Text / Etherpad as the agent substrate — they're server-authoritative editors, awkward to make an LLM a true CRDT peer in; fine for human-only embedded pads, not for agent-as-peer co-drafting.
Mixing tree-structure and in-node text conflict resolution naively — keep them as independent transform/merge domains (block ops vs text-in-block) to avoid combinatorial blowup.
Forgetting to sign agent ops — without per-agent capauth signatures you lose the ability to distinguish/audit Lumina's edits in a federated room.
bestPractices2027: AI agent = server-side CRDT peer with its own doc instance + awareness presence (cursor + thinking/composing/idle status), indistinguishable on the wire from a human peer.
read->locate->edit tool loop with semantic search_text returning relative-position handles; never raw offsets.
Streaming-token-to-CRDT-op pipeline (incremental markdown/block parser) so agent output is structured rich text, not escaped strings.
Suggestion/tracked-changes mode as default; live direct co-write as an explicit opt-in toggle per document/room.
Resumable, offset-addressable transport (Durable Streams / Hocuspocus catch-up) with separate logical channels for doc, awareness, and chat — so offline + federated peers reconcile.
Agent-layer operation batching + rate-limited broadcast + server-side compaction to absorb agent write speed and bound CRDT metadata.
Encode human selection/cursor as relative anchors and attach to the agent prompt so 'edit this' targets the exact intended node under concurrency.
Shared single CRDT core in Rust (yrs/loro) reused across server and clients via FFI/WASM to guarantee identical merge semantics everywhere.

research.dimension: Meeting recording → diarized transcription → LLM minutes/summary pipeline (LiveKit egress + faster-whisper + qwen), with sovereign/on-device, consent, and storage
keyFindings: Two recording modes in LiveKit Egress map cleanly onto two product goals. ROOM COMPOSITE runs a headless Chrome to render the room as one MP4 (what viewers saw — good for a replayable artifact) but is heavy/transcoding. TRACK egress exports each track WITHOUT transcoding (Opus→Ogg) and, critically, can stream a single AUDIO track to a WebSocket as raw pcm_s16le @48kHz. The WebSocket also carries JSON control frames like {"muted":true}. Per-track audio is the right input for diarized transcription because each participant is a separate stream — you get speaker labels essentially for free from the track→participant mapping, no acoustic diarization guesswork.
Egress caveat: track egress can record the WRONG track if a server-side subscription manager calls UpdateSubscriptions on the egress participant. Pin subscriptions / use a dedicated egress identity.

The 2026 consensus ASR stack is pyannote (who-spoke-when) + faster-whisper (what-was-said), with WhisperX as the integrated wrapper that adds VAD chunking, wav2vec2 forced alignment for word-level timestamps, and pyannote diarization. The recommended technique is NOT letting Whisper segment freely — use VAD/diarization timestamps to slice audio, transcribe per-segment, then align word-level Whisper timestamps (word_timestamps=True) to speaker turns for per-word attribution.

Per-participant track egress LARGELY OBVIATES acoustic diarization. If each speaker already has their own clean audio track, you label by track identity and only need pyannote when a single track contains multiple voices (e.g., a phone/room mic / one device with several people). This is a major sovereign-stack simplification vs commercial single-mixed-stream tools.

Model choice 2026: faster-whisper large-v3 for max quality, large-v3-turbo for backlog/speed at modest accuracy cost. VRAM is sequential-not-simultaneous best practice: run transcription, THEN diarization in a separate process to avoid co-resident OOM (relevant since the .100 box is already VRAM-near-limit per the qwen3.6 notes).

The LLM-minutes layer best practice is map-reduce over speaker-labelled transcript: chunk to ~10-15 min segments, summarize each (map), then a final reduce prompt that emits a STRUCTURED schema (overview/attendees, decisions[], action_items[{owner,task,due_date,priority,status}], risks/open_questions, next_steps). Speaker labels like [Speaker 0]/names are what let the LLM correctly attribute action items to owners.

Reliable structured output uses a 4-layer prompt: (1) explicit JSON schema with types, (2) one perfect few-shot example, (3) strict formatting rules, (4) a self-validation instruction. Cap list sizes (e.g. max 10 action items) to fight runaway extraction. qwen at .100:8082 already runs OpenAI /v1 API with 64k ctx — enough to often do single-pass reduce on a 60-90 min meeting, but map-reduce still wins on long/multi-topic calls.

UX pattern divergence: GRANOLA = no bot, no recording announcement, captures device audio, user jots rough notes during the call and AI ENHANCES them post-call (best for trust-sensitive/external). OTTER/FIREFLIES = a visible bot participant joins (acceptable for internal, enables CRM auto-push). Zoom AI Companion / Teams recap are native-platform recaps. The market verdict: no single tool serves both trust-sensitive and data-pipeline cases.

Zoom AI Companion's action-item extraction works by parsing imperative/assignment phrasing ('Jake, send the wireframes by Thursday' → action item owned by Jake, due Thursday) and pushing to Zoom Tasks with owner+deadline. 'Custom Briefs' let users re-tailor the same summary for different audiences (internal short vs client-detailed) — a reusable UX idea: one transcript, multiple prompt-driven views.

Consent is a legal gate, not a nicety. Many US states (CA, etc.) are two-/all-party consent for audio; video doesn't change the audio rule and it applies to Zoom/Teams/Meet equally. The standard mitigation is a recording disclaimer shown when recording starts / on join, with stay-or-leave consent. Self-hosting removes GDPR cross-border-transfer risk because audio never leaves your perimeter.

Meetily (Zackriya-Solutions, Rust, 100% local) is the closest open-source reference architecture to a sovereign build: captures system audio locally, Parakeet/Whisper live transcription (claims 4x faster), local speaker diarization (WhisperX-style, in active dev), and Ollama LLM summarization — explicitly 'no cloud required.' Validates the exact faster-whisper + local-LLM shape you already run.
whatToBorrow: LiveKit TRACK egress over WebSocket streaming raw pcm_s16le@48kHz per audio track → feed directly into faster-whisper at .100:18794. Skip room-composite for the transcription path; optionally ALSO fire a room-composite egress to MP4 if a replayable artifact is wanted. Use livekit egress AutoEgress so recording starts automatically when the room is created/flagged.

Per-participant track identity → speaker label mapping. Since skchat is an SFU-on-tailnet (LiveKit), each agent/human is already a distinct track — derive speaker names from LiveKit participant identity instead of running acoustic diarization. Only invoke pyannote when one track is known to be multi-voice.
WhisperX-style alignment (word_timestamps=True + forced alignment) for clean per-speaker, per-word transcripts; keep large-v3-turbo for live/backlog and large-v3 for archival quality.

Map-reduce summarization on qwen .100:8082 with a strict JSON schema: {title, date, attendees[], decisions[], action_items[{owner,task,due_date,priority,status}], open_questions[], next_steps[]}. Use the 4-layer structured-output prompt (schema + example + rules + self-validate) and cap list lengths. qwen's 64k ctx allows single-pass for most calls; fall back to map-reduce >~60 min.

Granola's 'augment my notes' hybrid: let the participant (or the agent) drop rough notes during the call; the post-call LLM pass MERGES human notes + transcript rather than summarizing transcript alone — dramatically higher-fidelity minutes. Fits skchat's agent-in-the-room model: Lumina can take live notes.

Zoom 'Custom Briefs': store the transcript once, generate multiple audience-tailored summaries on demand (internal terse / client / decision-log) by swapping the reduce prompt. Cheap because the expensive ASR step is already done.

Zoom-style action-item attribution heuristic in the prompt: explicitly instruct the model to detect assignment/imperative phrasing and bind owner+due_date; emit owners as participant identities so they can be routed (e.g. into skcapstone coord tasks / GTD capture).

Meetily as the architectural reference repo — Rust local-first capture + Whisper + Ollama summarization + local diarization; mine its pipeline structure (live transcription buffering, diarization-as-separate-process) since it solves the same problem you're solving.
Recording disclaimer/consent flow: a stay-or-leave consent banner when egress starts, mirroring Zoom's customizable disclaimer; record the consent event in the meeting metadata.
recommendationsForUs: Build the pipeline as: LiveKit Track Egress (WebSocket, audio-only, pcm_s16le 48kHz, per participant) → faster-whisper .100:18794 (large-v3-turbo live / large-v3 for final) → per-track transcript merged + interleaved by timestamp into a single speaker-labelled transcript (speaker = LiveKit participant identity) → qwen .100:8082 map-reduce minutes with strict JSON schema → write minutes to skmem-pg docs table (mxbai-embedded, so minutes are searchable via hybrid_search_docs) and surface in skchat.

Make 'minutes by default' the UX: when a room is created with record=true (or an agent/host toggles 'Record & summarize'), fire AutoEgress for the transcription track stream AND set a post-call hook that runs the qwen reduce automatically and DMs/posts the minutes back into the skchat room + sk-alert the host. No separate 'generate summary' button — the artifact appears when the call ends. This is the Granola/Otter table-stakes expectation.

Run diarization (pyannote) ONLY as a fallback for tracks flagged multi-voice; default path uses track identity for zero-cost speaker labels. This sidesteps the pyannote HF-gated-model 401 friction and the dual-model VRAM pressure noted in your qwen3.6 VRAM-near-limit memory.

Respect the .100 VRAM constraint: qwen3.6 is near the VRAM ceiling and --parallel 1 serializes. Do NOT co-load pyannote+whisper+qwen simultaneously — run transcription to completion, free VRAM, then summarize. faster-whisper (CTranslate2) and qwen contend; consider running the whisper service and the summarize pass at different times, or pin whisper to CPU/iGPU if it competes (note: Arc iGPU corrupts generation per your memory, so keep whisper on the 5060 Ti or CPU, not Arc).

Add a consent gate to skchat recording: show a stay-or-leave disclaimer when egress starts, store a consent record in room metadata, and default-deny recording in rooms with external/guest participants unless explicitly accepted (matches the sovereign-conf-calls guest/sovereign split already in your plan).

Store transcript + minutes + (optional) composite MP4 with a retention policy and ownership tag; since everything is on-tailnet/sovereign you avoid GDPR cross-border issues, but still set per-room retention and make minutes the durable artifact (cheap, searchable) while raw audio/video is short-retention.

Route extracted action_items into existing infra: each {owner,task,due_date} → skcapstone coord_create or gtd_capture, so meeting minutes are actionable not just archival. This is the differentiator commercial tools charge for (Fireflies CRM push / Zoom Tasks).

Reuse the existing skchat transcribe_audio_file MCP tool path and capture_chat_to_memory pattern — wire the egress→whisper→qwen output through capture_to_memory so minutes land in skmem-pg automatically.
Offer Granola-style live note augmentation: let the in-room agent (Lumina) or host jot notes during the call; merge them into the reduce prompt for higher-fidelity minutes.
bestPractices2027: Per-track audio capture > single mixed stream — preserve speaker separation at the source (SFU track identity) instead of recovering it via acoustic diarization.
ASR = pyannote/VAD-sliced + faster-whisper transcription + forced-alignment word timestamps; never rely on Whisper's native segmentation for diarized output.
Summarization = map-reduce over speaker-labelled transcript with strict JSON schema + 4-layer structured-output prompting + self-validation + capped list sizes.
Summary-by-default UX: the minutes/recap is an automatic artifact produced when recording is enabled and the call ends — not a manual action.
Sequential VRAM usage (transcribe, free, then summarize) on shared GPUs; diarization as a separate process.
Consent-first: recording disclaimer with stay-or-leave on egress start; self-host to eliminate cross-border data-transfer exposure.
One transcript, many views (Zoom Custom Briefs) — regenerate audience-tailored summaries on demand from the stored transcript.
Close the loop: convert extracted action items into real tasks (owner+due) in your task system, not just text.
Bot-free / device-or-SFU-native capture (Granola model) for trust-sensitive calls; visible-bot capture only where its presence is acceptable.
pitfalls: Track egress recording the WRONG track when a server-side subscription manager calls UpdateSubscriptions on the egress participant — use a dedicated egress identity / pinned subscriptions.
Co-loading pyannote + whisper + qwen exhausts VRAM (documented on 24GB 4090 at batch_size=16); your .100 is already VRAM-near-limit and serialized (--parallel 1) — run models sequentially.
pyannote 3.x throws cryptic 401 Unauthorized if the two gated HF models' terms aren't accepted — a real blocker on a fresh sovereign box; prefer track-identity labels to avoid it where possible.
Arc iGPU corrupts LLM generation (your own root-cause note) — don't try to offload whisper or summarize to Arc; keep on 5060 Ti or CPU.
mxbai embedding ctx=512 truncates ~1100 chars — when storing minutes in skmem-pg, chunk minutes before embedding or you lose recall on long minutes.
Letting Whisper free-segment then naively assigning speakers yields drifting attribution; align word timestamps to speaker turns.
LLM summary hallucinating action items / owners not actually stated — cap list size, require source-grounding, and only attribute owners when explicitly named (Zoom's 'when clearly stated' rule).
Two-/all-party consent legal exposure if recording external participants without a notice — applies to audio even on video calls; bake a consent gate in before shipping.
Single-pass summarization on very long calls overruns even 64k ctx or degrades mid-context — use map-reduce past ~60-90 min.
Treating raw audio/video as the durable artifact — it's expensive and high-retention-risk; the searchable minutes + transcript should be the durable artifact, raw media short-retention.

research.dimension: Plugin/module architecture for an extensible app (Flutter client + skcomms backend): module manifest, registry, contribution points (toolbar/screens/services), settings-driven enable + placement, tied to the live /api/v1/capabilities service-discovery doc
keyFindings: Five reference systems converge on the SAME three-part pattern: (1) a static declarative MANIFEST, (2) lazy ACTIVATION, (3) CONTRIBUTION POINTS that name where UI/behavior plugs in. VS Code: package.json `contributes` + `activationEvents` (since 1.74 a declared `command` auto-activates, no explicit `onCommand` needed). Obsidian: manifest.json (id, minAppVersion) + a Plugin.onload() that calls add*/register* (addRibbonIcon, addStatusBarItem, registerView, addSettingTab) where EVERY add*/register* is auto-cleaned-up on unload. Nextcloud: appinfo/info.xml `<navigation>` entries with name/order/route/role(all|admin|settings) + enabled/disabled per-instance state. Matrix widgets: declarative widget def + runtime capability NEGOTIATION (requestCapabilities -> driver/user approval) over a postMessage WidgetApi/WidgetDriver host-guest split. Flutter: a registry+interface in a core package, plugins register at startup, plus `deferred as` imports / Play Feature Delivery for on-demand code loading.

The decisive idea to steal is VS Code's separation of `when` (per-placement VISIBILITY) from `enablement` (global AVAILABILITY). Map directly onto skchat: enablement = does the node's /api/v1/capabilities support this module's required service/transport; when/placement = user settings choosing whether it shows as a bottom-nav tab, a toolbar icon, or only in the swipe-up drawer. This is exactly the 'maps onto main toolbar' vs 'in the drawer' requirement.

skchat-app ALREADY has the hard parts half-built: lib/services/capabilities_service.dart fully parses the live `/api/v1/capabilities` doc into NodeCapabilities{transports[], services[]} with a CapStatus enum (up/configured/degraded/down/unconfigured), exposed via nodeCapabilitiesProvider (Riverpod FutureProvider). The skcomms side (skcomms/src/skcomms/capabilities.py build_capabilities) emits service ids: text, voice, video, file-transfer, data-streaming, federation, access-plane(sk-access), geo-cot(cot) and transports: file/syncthing/https-s2s/websocket/tailscale/webrtc/p2p/ble-mesh/lora/nostr. A module manifest's `requires` field can reference these exact ids verbatim — no new backend contract needed for the first cut.

The anti-pattern to fix is concrete and present: AppShell._tabs is a hard-coded const list of 5 _TabItem; app_router.dart hard-codes ~30 GoRoute entries; HubScreen is a hand-maintained list of _OpsTile that duplicates the route list. Adding/removing a feature today means editing 3 files in lockstep. skmap is the perfect first migration target — it's already a self-contained feature (lib/features/skmap/, route /skmap, only reachable via a hand-coded Hub tile) and the user explicitly wants to promote it onto the toolbar.

Flutter has no true runtime code sandboxing for first-party Dart (a Dart plugin is compiled in — `deferred as` only defers loading, not isolation). Genuine third-party/untrusted sandboxing in this stack is achievable ONLY for web-style modules via an iframe + postMessage bridge (the Matrix widget model) or by keeping the module out-of-process behind skcomms-api/capauth. So the realistic model is: TWO tiers — (A) compiled-in first-party 'modules' governed by manifest + capability gating + settings (no isolation, trusted), and (B) optional 'widget'-style external surfaces (iframe/WebView on web, capauth-scoped REST on native) for anything untrusted.

Settings persistence precedent already exists: theme_provider.dart and daemon_config.dart show the app's pattern (Riverpod provider backed by hive_flutter, which is already in pubspec). A ModulePrefs store (enabled set + placement map + order) should reuse hive, not introduce a new dependency.
whatToBorrow: VS Code `contributes` + when/enablement split — model the manifest's contribution points as named slots: `nav` (bottom tab), `toolbar` (app-bar action — the maps-on-toolbar case), `drawer` (the swipe-up all-apps list), `screen` (a routable GoRoute), `settingsTab`. Borrow `group@order` string syntax for ordering and the `when`-context-key idea, but feed the context keys from capabilities + settings instead of editor state.

Obsidian's auto-cleanup register* lifecycle — give each module an onActivate(ModuleContext)/onDispose() where everything it registers (routes, providers, toolbar items) is tracked and torn down on disable, so toggling a module off in settings fully removes it with no leak. Also borrow Obsidian's single flat manifest.json with id + minAppVersion (-> minDaemonApi / minAppVersion) for compatibility gating.

Nextcloud info.xml `<navigation>` with order + role + per-instance enabled/disabled — directly model placement entries with `order`, a `role` (everyone vs operator-only, matching the existing Ops/operator surfaces), and an installed-but-disabled state. Nextcloud's 'bump version so settings re-register on update' is a good migration discipline.

Matrix widget capability NEGOTIATION (requestCapabilities -> approval) — adopt for the untrusted tier-B surfaces only: a module declares `requires: [service:geo-cot, transport:webrtc]`; the host checks the live capabilities doc AND (for sensitive scopes like file/access-plane) prompts the operator once, mirroring capauth scoping. Use the WidgetApi/WidgetDriver postMessage split as the literal design for any iframe-hosted module on web.

Flutter `deferred as` imports + go_router lazy route builders — wrap heavy module screen imports (skmap/flutter_map, livekit, conf) in deferred imports so disabled modules don't inflate cold start, exactly the on-demand-feature-module pattern. Keep flutter_modular OFF the table (it fights go_router, which the app already standardizes on) — build a thin custom registry instead.

flutter_riverpod registry pattern — expose the whole thing as `moduleRegistryProvider` (the manifests) + `enabledModulesProvider` (registry filtered by capabilities ∩ settings) + `navModulesProvider`/`toolbarModulesProvider`/`drawerModulesProvider` (placement-filtered, watched by AppShell). This matches the app's existing Riverpod-everywhere style (nodeCapabilitiesProvider, themeProvider).
recommendationsForUs: Define a ModuleManifest Dart model in a new lib/core/modules/module_manifest.dart with fields: id (e.g. 'skmap'), title, icon, route (GoRoute path), version, minDaemonApi, requires (List<CapabilityRef> like 'service:geo-cot','transport:webrtc'), defaultPlacement (nav|toolbar|drawer), role (everyone|operator), order, and a builder/onActivate hook. Keep it a const-constructible class so first-party modules are declared in code (compiled-in tier), not loaded from disk.

Build lib/core/modules/module_registry.dart holding a const List<ModuleManifest> _builtinModules (migrate the current 30 routes + 5 tabs + Hub tiles into manifests). Expose moduleRegistryProvider. Add a capability gate: enabledModulesProvider = ref.watch(nodeCapabilitiesProvider) filtered so a module only lights up when EVERY `requires` ref resolves to a CapStatus of up/configured/degraded in the live doc — reuse the existing capabilities_service.dart parsing verbatim. Modules whose caps are down/unconfigured render greyed in the drawer (honest, like the existing status-dot mapping) rather than vanishing.

Replace AppShell._tabs (const) with `ref.watch(navModulesProvider)` and add a swipe-up 'all apps' drawer fed by `ref.watch(drawerModulesProvider)` (a DraggableScrollableSheet listing every enabled module grouped by role). Add a toolbar contribution slot in the per-screen AppBar fed by `toolbarModulesProvider` — this is what lets the user promote SkMap onto the main toolbar. AppShell._indexFor's hard-coded location.startsWith() chain should be derived from the manifest list instead of inlined.

Drive routing from the registry: in app_router.dart, generate the shell GoRoute children by mapping over the manifests (route + deferred builder) so adding a module no longer means editing app_router.dart. Keep the top-level non-shell routes (calls, viewer, join) hand-written — they're not user-toggleable modules.

Add a ModulePrefs settings store (lib/services/module_prefs.dart) backed by hive (already a dep, same pattern as theme_provider/daemon_config): persist {enabledIds:Set, placement:Map<id,nav|toolbar|drawer>, order:Map<id,int>}. Add a 'Modules' settings tab under the Me/profile screen (where capabilities_section.dart already lives) with a list of all manifests: a toggle (enable/disable, disabled-if-capability-unsupported with an explanatory subtitle pulled from the cap status) and a placement chooser (nav / toolbar / drawer) + drag-to-reorder. Settings ∩ capabilities = final enabled set.

Backend: extend skcomms/src/skcomms/capabilities.py to optionally emit a `modules` block alongside transports/services — a per-node list of module ids the NODE wants surfaced (operator policy / which sub-apps this deployment ships), so the client can honor node-side enablement, not just client-side. Keep it additive (the existing transports/services contract is untouched; the Flutter parser already null-tolerates missing keys). Version the doc with a top-level `api` field so manifests' minDaemonApi can gate.

Pilot with ONE module end-to-end before generalizing: migrate skmap (self-contained, route /skmap, requires:['service:geo-cot'], defaultPlacement: drawer) into a manifest, delete its hand-coded Hub tile, prove the toggle + promote-to-toolbar + capability-gating loop, then mechanically migrate the remaining features (skos, coord, cluster, recordings, conf, spaces). Use the superpowers TDD/brainstorming skills and write a spec under docs/superpowers/specs/ first (the repo's established practice).

Reserve a tier-B 'widget' escape hatch for the future without building it now: document that untrusted/external sub-apps would mount via an iframe (web) / WebView (native) + a postMessage capability-negotiation bridge modeled on matrix-widget-api, scoped by capauth — so the manifest's `requires`/`role` fields are forward-compatible with that approval flow.
bestPractices2027: Declarative manifest + lazy activation + named contribution points is the cross-ecosystem standard (VS Code, Obsidian, Nextcloud, JetBrains all share it) — don't invent a novel model.

Separate AVAILABILITY (capability/permission-gated, global) from VISIBILITY/PLACEMENT (user-settings-driven, per-slot) — the VS Code enablement-vs-when distinction; conflating them produces brittle UIs.
Auto-track and tear down everything a module registers (Obsidian register* model) so enable/disable is leak-free and reversible at runtime.
Gate features on RUNTIME capability discovery, not build flags — a module lights up only when the node actually supports its required service/transport (service-discovery-driven UI).
Use deferred/dynamic imports so disabled or rarely-used modules don't tax cold start (Flutter deferred components / on-demand feature modules).

For untrusted code, the only real sandbox in a Flutter/web stack is iframe+postMessage (Matrix widget) or out-of-process behind an authz boundary — compiled-in Dart is trusted by definition; design the two tiers explicitly.
Capability negotiation with explicit user/operator approval for sensitive scopes (Matrix requestCapabilities -> approval), rather than implicit grant.
Version the manifest/host contract (minAppVersion / minDaemonApi) and re-register on version bump (Nextcloud) so upgrades don't silently drop contributions.
pitfalls: Adopting flutter_modular for the registry — it owns routing and DI and conflicts with go_router, which skchat-app already standardizes on (go_router 14, ShellRoute in app_router.dart). Build a thin custom registry over go_router instead.
Believing `deferred as` gives sandboxing/isolation — it only defers code download/parse; a compiled-in Dart module has full app access. Don't market tier-A modules as sandboxed.

Letting capability-gating HIDE modules silently — the existing app philosophy (capabilities.py docstring, CapStatus dot mapping) is HONESTY: show down/unconfigured modules greyed with a reason, don't make them vanish, or users think features are missing/broken.

Manifest/route/Hub-tile drift — today a feature lives in app_shell._tabs, app_router routes, AND HubScreen tiles; if the migration leaves any of these hand-maintained the same 3-file-edit problem returns. Generate nav + routes + drawer from the single manifest list.

Over-engineering a disk-loaded plugin format for v1 — all current sub-apps are first-party and compiled-in; a JSON-on-disk loader adds risk with no user today. Start with const manifests in Dart; add external loading only when a tier-B widget actually exists.

Breaking the /api/v1/capabilities contract — the Flutter parser and skcomms emitter are already in production (Me screen capabilities_section). Any `modules` addition must be additive and null-tolerant; don't restructure transports/services.
Ignoring the role dimension — several surfaces are operator-only (Ops hub, cluster, skos-control). Placement/visibility must respect a role field or non-operators get controls they shouldn't see.

Putting the swipe-up drawer over the existing PiPOverlay / incoming-call push in AppShell without coordinating z-order and the GoRouterState.of(context) listener — the shell already juggles call overlays and offline banners.

research.dimension: Location/maps sharing + high-value chat modules (pin-drop, live location, polls, events, whiteboard) as pluggable modules in skchat
keyFindings: Two distinct location primitives exist in every mature chat app and should be modeled separately: (1) STATIC pin-drop = a single immutable message (Matrix m.location / MSC3488: a geo: RFC5870 URI 'geo:lat,lng;u=accuracy', optional description, m.ts, and m.asset type m.self vs m.pin) and (2) LIVE location = a long-lived, periodically-edited object (Telegram inputMediaGeoLive; Matrix m.beacon_info/MSC3489+3672).

Telegram's live-location design is the cleanest reference: start with inputMediaGeoLive carrying geo_point + accuracy_radius + heading(1-360deg) + period (validity/expiry) + proximity_notification_radius; the SAME message is updated in-place via messages.editMessage at most every `period` seconds; STOP is an explicit edit with inputGeoPointEmpty + stopped=true. Live sharing is identical in 1:1 and groups (broadcast to all members).

Live location is an EDIT-of-one-message, not a stream of new messages. This is the critical design choice: it keeps timelines clean, gives natural expiry, makes 'stop' explicit, and means a single pin animates on the map rather than spamming the chat. Matrix's m.beacon_info (state event holding metadata/expiry) + separate m.beacon location pulses follows the same split.

Telegram 2026 reduced live update cadence from 10s -> 2s and added an 'Until I stop' indefinite option alongside the classic 15min / 1h / 8h durations. Cadence should be adaptive (faster when moving/heading present, slower when stationary) to save battery and bandwidth.

Proximity alerts are a high-value, low-cost add-on: each live share carries an optional proximity_notification_radius; when another live-sharer in the same chat crosses it, the system emits a service/system message (Telegram messageActionGeoProximityReached, includes distance). This needs no extra UI surface, just server-side distance checks on each pulse.

GDPR/2026 regulatory posture makes opt-in mandatory and engineering-enforced: consent must be affirmative, specific, separate from other permissions, revocable as easily as granted, with contextual (just-in-time) disclosure, data minimization, and changes that 'propagate immediately to every system that touches the data.' Opt-out-by-default location is non-compliant.

skchat already has the exact extension surface needed: a PluginRegistry with on_outbound/on_inbound/on_command(slash) hooks (src/skchat/plugins.py), a ChatMessage envelope with a content_type enum + an extensible metadata dict (src/skchat/models.py), and an AttachmentService transport selector. But ContentType is currently text-only (PLAIN/MARKDOWN/SYSTEM) — it has no structured/typed-payload concept, which is the main gap for any module (location, poll, event).

The repo already has the two hard pieces most chat apps lack: a geo/map surface (skmap) and a CoT/TAK tactical bridge (cursor-on-target, ATAK/iTAK on the sovereign net per MEMORY). A location module is therefore mostly a wiring + privacy-policy job, not a from-scratch build — and live-location pulses can dual-publish as CoT PLI (Position Location Information) events to feed ATAK directly.

Highest-value non-location modules across WhatsApp/Telegram/Matrix/Slack in 2026, ranked by value/effort: polls (Matrix MSC3381 m.poll.start/m.poll.response — server aggregates votes, only latest vote per user counts, no fallback render so dumb clients ignore), events/scheduling (RSVP objects), file/media sharing (skchat already has AttachmentService), and collaborative whiteboard (highest effort — needs a CRDT).
whatToBorrow: Telegram live-location wire model verbatim as the skchat live-location payload: {geo:{lat,lng}, accuracy_radius_m, heading_deg(1-360|null), period_s|null(=indefinite), proximity_radius_m|null, stopped:bool}. Update via message-edit, terminate via stopped=true. Source: https://core.telegram.org/api/live-location and constructor inputMediaGeoLive.

Matrix MSC3488 m.location content for the STATIC pin: store the point as an RFC5870 geo: URI ('geo:37.78,-122.40;u=35') plus a `description` and `m.asset`:{type:'m.self'|'m.pin'} so 'my location' vs 'a dropped pin' are distinguishable. Carry a text/markdown fallback so non-location clients still render something. Source: matrix-spec-proposals 3488-location.md.

Matrix MSC3489/3672 'beacon' split: one durable beacon_info object (owner, expiry, live=true/false, description) + lightweight periodic location pulses keyed to it. Use MSC3672's ephemeral/non-persisted variant when history isn't wanted (privacy default). Source: matrix-spec-proposals PRs 3489, 3672.

Matrix MSC3381 polls model for a polls module: m.poll.start {question, kind: disclosed|undisclosed, max_selections, answers:[{id,text}]} + m.poll.response {answers:[ids]} where the SERVER aggregates and only the latest response per user (by ts) counts, and responses have no renderable fallback (dumb clients ignore them). Source: matrix-spec-proposals/proposals/3381-polls.md.

Telegram proximity_notification_radius + the messageActionGeoProximityReached system-message pattern (carries the crossing distance) — implement as a server-side check on each live pulse, emitting a ContentType.SYSTEM message into the chat. Cheap, high delight for groups/meetups/tactical use.

CoT/TAK PLI mapping: when a user shares live location, also emit a Cursor-on-Target event (uid, type a-f-G-U-C, point lat/lon/hae/ce/le, contact callsign, stale time = period) onto the existing CoT bridge so ATAK/iTAK see chat participants as map icons. Live-location `period` maps directly to CoT `stale`.

GDPR-2026 consent UX pattern: just-in-time permission prompt separate from any other permission, a precise-vs-approximate toggle (coarse rounds to ~city/neighborhood = data minimization), explicit duration picker (15m/1h/8h/until-stop), an always-visible 'currently sharing with N people, stop' banner, and revoke-as-easy-as-grant. Sources: secureprivacy.ai app-privacy-compliance-guide, termsfeed user-location-privacy-practices.
recommendationsForUs: Build location as a first-class typed message, not a freeform attachment. Extend skchat ContentType (src/skchat/models.py) with LOCATION='application/skchat.location+json' (static pin) and LOCATION_LIVE='application/skchat.location-live+json', and put the structured payload in the existing ChatMessage.metadata dict so the SKComms transport envelope and E2E encryption are unchanged. Always set a human-readable text/markdown fallback in body so non-location clients degrade gracefully (the Matrix extensible-events principle).

Implement live location as edit-of-one-message + expiry, NOT a stream of new messages: create one 'live beacon' message, then have the sender push location pulses that UPDATE it (mirror Telegram messages.editMessage and the existing edit/reaction plumbing). Store {started_at, expires_at, last_seen, live:bool} so the map can show 'last updated 12s ago' and auto-expire. This keeps the timeline clean and makes 'stop' explicit (set live=false / stopped=true).

Ship it as a skchat plugin (src/skchat/plugins.py PluginRegistry) named e.g. skchat-location: register slash commands /location (pin), /live <15m|1h|8h|until-stop> [precise|approx], /stopshare via on_command; render incoming LOCATION* messages into the skmap surface via on_inbound. This matches the SKWorld host-plugin pattern (owned core + thin adapter) so it survives skchat updates and can be enabled per-deployment.

Make sharing strictly opt-in with these enforced defaults: NO location is ever sent without an explicit per-share action; default precision = APPROXIMATE (coarse/rounded ~city-block) with an explicit per-share 'precise' toggle; default duration is bounded (offer 15m/1h/8h, make 'until I stop' a deliberate extra tap); a persistent 'You are sharing live location with N people — Stop' banner; one-tap revoke; and an audit log entry per share start/stop (you already have security_audit_log via the skcapstone MCP — write to it).

Add adaptive pulse cadence with battery/bandwidth guards: ~2-5s when moving (heading present / position delta > threshold), back off to 30-60s when stationary, hard-stop at expires_at. Carry accuracy_radius and heading so skmap renders an accuracy circle + direction cone like the reference apps.

Wire proximity alerts and the CoT bridge in from day one since both surfaces already exist: on each live pulse, run a server-side distance check against other live-sharers in the same group and emit a ContentType.SYSTEM 'X is within Ym' message (opt-in radius per share); and dual-publish live pulses as CoT PLI events to the TAK bridge (period -> CoT stale) so chat participants appear as ATAK map icons. This is a differentiator no consumer app has.

Sequence the non-location modules by value/effort: (1) polls next — small, well-specified (adopt MSC3381 server-side aggregation, latest-vote-wins, no-fallback-render), reuses the same typed-payload+plugin pattern; (2) events/scheduling — a typed m.event-like payload {title, start, end, location(optional, can embed a pin), rsvp:[]} with /event + RSVP reactions, integrate with the existing gog calendar + sk-alert paths; (3) file/media is mostly done via AttachmentService — just add gallery/thumbnail + delete UI; (4) whiteboard LAST — it needs a CRDT (Yjs/Automerge) over a data channel, defer unless there's clear demand.

Define ONE generic 'typed module message' contract so every future module is plug-and-play: content_type = application/skchat.<module>+json, payload in metadata, mandatory text fallback, optional system-message side-effects, and a plugin that owns render(on_inbound)/compose(slash on_command)/aggregate(server). Location is the first instance; polls/events follow the identical shape. This avoids a bespoke code path per module.
bestPractices2027: Model static pin and live location as two different object types — static = immutable message (geo: URI + asset type), live = a single editable/expiring beacon object updated in place, never a stream of timeline messages.

Live location is opt-in, time-boxed, and revocable: bounded default durations, a deliberate 'until I stop', an always-visible active-sharing indicator, and stop/revoke as easy as start (GDPR-2026 mandate).
Precision is a user choice with a privacy-preserving default: ship approximate/coarse by default, precise only on explicit per-share opt-in (data minimization).
Adaptive update cadence (faster moving, slower stationary, hard expiry) — Telegram moved to 2s when active in 2026 but you must back off to preserve battery/bandwidth.
Carry accuracy_radius + heading and render them (accuracy circle + direction cone) instead of a bare dot, so users can judge trust in the fix.
Just-in-time, layered consent presented at the moment of sharing and separate from other permissions; log each share start/stop to an audit trail.
Use extensible/typed events with a plain-text fallback so clients that don't understand a module degrade gracefully rather than break (Matrix MSC1767 extensible-events principle).
Server-aggregated state for collaborative modules (polls: latest-vote-wins, server tallies; proximity: server-side distance checks) rather than trusting each client to recompute.
pitfalls: Sending live location as a stream of new messages (instead of editing one beacon) — floods the timeline, breaks notifications, and makes 'stop' and expiry ambiguous. Telegram and Matrix both deliberately avoid this.
Opt-out-by-default or auto-sharing — non-compliant under GDPR-2026 (consent must be affirmative/specific) and a trust killer. Background/continuous location especially must be explicit and explained.
Defaulting to precise location — leaks more than needed; coarse-by-default with explicit precise opt-in is the data-minimization expectation.
No hard expiry / no visible active-share indicator — leads to users unknowingly broadcasting indefinitely (the classic live-location privacy incident).
Fixed high-frequency pulse cadence — drains battery and saturates the transport when stationary; needs adaptive backoff.
Letting any user edit another user's live-location/beacon state event — the exact permission flaw flagged against early Matrix MSC3489 (use 'owned' state events / per-sender keys).

Building location as an opaque attachment blob — loses the typed payload, map rendering, expiry, and proximity logic; and gives no clean path to reuse for polls/events. Use a typed content_type + structured metadata instead.
skchat's current ContentType is text-only — shipping a module without first introducing a typed-payload contract will create a bespoke, un-reusable code path per module.
Whiteboard without a CRDT — naive last-write-wins sync produces lost strokes and conflicts; it genuinely needs Yjs/Automerge over a data channel, which is why it should be sequenced last.
Proximity alerts without per-share opt-in radius — surfacing distances between users by default is a privacy leak; the radius must be set deliberately by the sharer.

gaps: E2EE for media is NOT implemented. connectivity.py/livekit only do transport security (DTLS-SRTP) + sovereign relay; the SFU can see all media. 2026 best practice = LiveKit Insertable-Streams E2EE (per-room or MLS/MEGOLM key provider, AES-GCM, ~0 server overhead). NOTE the tradeoff that bites US directly: server-side egress recording AND the AI-agent participants (lumina-livekit-agent, recording_writeup transcription) CANNOT read E2EE media — so we'd need a hybrid (E2EE for human-only rooms, opt-out when an agent/recorder is present, with explicit consent UI). This is the single biggest security gap for a sovereign suite.

Collaborative docs are last-write-wins, not CRDT. doc_panel.dart explicitly says 'simple last-write-wins (not CRDT)'. 2026 standard = Yjs (Y.Text/Y.Map, awareness protocol for cursors/presence, offline merge). No conflict-free merge, no multi-cursor, no rich text, and critically no 'agent as a CRDT peer' (the 2026 pattern for agent-collaborative editing — agent streams CRDT ops, not text diffs). The whiteboard panel likely has the same limitation.

SkMap live geo feed is a stub. geo_units_source.dart:165 / skmap_providers.dart:23 have a single TODO each to call GET /api/v1/geo/units; that route + a server-side GeoStore (fed by the CoT bridge stream) do not exist, and there is no self-position publish path (operator/agent location is hardcoded to the LUMINA seed). Need: (a) a geo route/SSE-or-WS stream of application/geo+json or CoT envelopes on the skchat/skcomms daemon, (b) a publish-my-location endpoint, (c) live-beacon TTL/staleness. The transport+UI+CoT bridge all exist — only the wire is missing.

No unified group VIDEO grid for ad-hoc calls. Spaces are AUDIO-only (recording.py audio_only=True, room screen renders rings not video tiles); 1:1 has video; screenshare renders video. Missing: a multi-party camera-video tile grid (the 'group video on the fly' literal ask) as a first-class surface distinct from audio Spaces + the screen_share panel. Reuse VideoTrackRenderer + the LiveKit grant already supports video — it's a UI/role gap, not infra.

No LiveKit Agents framework integration for meeting intelligence. Our agent join (lumina-livekit-agent.py) is bespoke; recording_writeup is batch (post-call OGG->Whisper->LLM). 2026 best practice = livekit/agents 1.5 (MCP-native, adaptive interruption) for LIVE in-call transcription captions + live minutes + agent that can speak/act mid-meeting. We have the pieces (qwen3.6, Whisper, MCP tool router from the telegram bridge) but not the real-time in-room transcription protocol / live caption surface.

Screenshare and watch-together tracks are not recorded or written-up. recording.py is audio_only; the egress doesn't capture the screenshare/whiteboard/doc lanes, so 'minutes' miss everything visual/shared. Consider video room-composite egress (with the E2EE caveat above) or lane-state snapshots folded into the write-up.

Spaces create/join trust the tailnet (S1/S2): routes.py /spaces/create comment says host_fqid is asserted not proven and it mints a roomAdmin token to whoever asks — 'Do NOT expose publicly before S5 sk-lk-authd verifies a capauth-signed operator assertion'. The federation /sfu/get path IS capauth-verified, but the local create/join path is not yet. Closing this is required before any public/guest exposure of Spaces.

No persistent server-side doc/whiteboard store beyond lane replay. Lanes persist to ~/.skchat/lanes.db (LaneStore replay) but there's no document model, versioning, naming, or export — a 'shared doc' evaporates with the Space. Need a docs feature (list/open/export, ownership) if docs are to be a first-class collaboration artifact rather than an ephemeral lane.
